marketPulse: Shock Absorbed, but Warflation Risk Still Dominates
marketPulseSubtext: Stocks finished mixed after absorbing an oil spike, while the dollar and yields stayed firm and volatility remained elevated.
topDriver: Iran war escalation keeps oil and inflation risk front and center
topDriverSubtext: Markets bent but did not break as crude surged above $109 Brent and $111 WTI, while investors still leaned on the dollar and kept pressure on rates.
disclaimer: Prototype disclaimer: This screen reflects a manual April 2, 2026 market snapshot, but it is not validated for trading, investment, or risk decisions. It would need to be hooked up to real-time market and news sources, then scored by tested algorithms and monitoring before production use.

topThemes.theme: Equities hold together better than the headlines
Dollar and yields confirm tighter financial conditions
Volatility stays elevated into the long weekend

rateCurveRead: As of the April 2 snapshot, Treasury yields moved higher across the curve instead of rallying on safety, which suggests the market is focusing more on inflation and supply shock risk than on a clean flight to duration.

crossMarket.driver: Broad market stabilized after early weakness as investors balanced high oil with late-session resilience
Growth recovered from intraday pressure and finished modestly higher
Cyclicals and travel-sensitive names felt the oil shock more directly
Safe-haven demand returned as investors sought dollar liquidity
Fed path firms as oil-driven inflation risk muddies the case for cuts
Inflation shock and term premium both leaning upward
Long-end stays under pressure as inflation uncertainty persists
Conflict and Hormuz uncertainty push the global benchmark sharply higher
U.S. crude posts its largest one-day dollar jump in decades
Higher yields and a stronger dollar undercut the metal despite geopolitical stress
Options market still prices headline risk and weekend uncertainty
Macro stress clipped risk appetite in crypto

drivers.name: Oil is now the transmission channel into everything else
drivers.detail: The biggest move of the day was crude, with WTI jumping above $111 and Brent closing above $109 as the market repriced the odds of a longer disruption tied to Iran and the Strait of Hormuz.

drivers.name: Stocks showed resilience, but not conviction
drivers.detail: Indexes finished only slightly mixed after sharp early losses, which says positioning and dip buying helped, but the market is still trading event risk rather than a durable all-clear.

drivers.name: Rates are flashing tighter conditions again
drivers.detail: The Treasury curve moved higher across maturities, which is a problem for equities, housing, and any part of the economy that needs lower financing costs to keep momentum.

drivers.name: The dollar is back in the conversation
drivers.detail: A stronger dollar is now reinforcing the shock by tightening global financial conditions and pressuring risk assets outside the narrow energy winners.
drivers.linked: Dollar index, global funding, FX sensitivity

drivers.name: The next areas to watch are transport, housing, and consumer demand
drivers.detail: Beyond oil itself, the most important spillovers are airline and freight sensitivity, mortgage-rate pressure, and whether higher fuel and financing costs begin to weigh on discretionary spending.
drivers.linked: Airlines, transports, mortgage rates, consumer sensitivity

risks.title: Oil shock bleeds into inflation expectations
risks.desc: If crude stays near or above current levels, the market may keep pushing rate-cut hopes further out and reprice inflation higher again.

risks.title: Long weekend headline gap risk
risks.desc: With markets closed for Good Friday, any geopolitical surprise could leave investors unable to react until the next reopening.

risks.title: Housing affordability keeps worsening
risks.desc: Mortgage rates have climbed again, which raises the odds that housing demand and broader consumer confidence soften further.

risks.title: False sense of equity resilience
risks.desc: A mixed close can mask fragility if the market is being carried by short covering while oil, yields, and volatility all stay elevated.

timeline.headline: President Trump's tougher Iran messaging revived fears of a longer conflict and another squeeze through the Strait of Hormuz.
timeline.effect: Oil surged, the dollar rebounded, and global risk sentiment turned defensive.

timeline.headline: U.S. stocks opened lower while volatility and Treasury yields pushed higher.
timeline.effect: The market repriced inflation risk more than it embraced a classic safety trade.

timeline.headline: Reports of Iran-Oman discussions on monitoring or reopening the strait helped calm the worst of the panic.
timeline.effect: Equities recovered from their lows and the session finished mixed instead of disorderly.

timeline.headline: Oil still finished sharply higher, while the dollar and rates stayed firm into the Good Friday shutdown.
timeline.effect: The close signaled resilience in stocks, but not resolution in the macro shock.

strategies.title: Watch Oil Against Yields
strategies.body: The key test is whether crude stays elevated and pulls Treasury yields even higher, because that combination is what tightens conditions fastest across the real economy.
strategies.trigger: WTI holds above roughly $110 while the 10Y remains near or above 4.35%

strategies.title: Monitor Transport and Travel Sensitivity
strategies.body: If airlines, shippers, and fuel-sensitive cyclicals keep underperforming, the market is telling you the oil shock is moving beyond commodities and into earnings risk.
strategies.trigger: Travel and transport groups lag even when the broad market stabilizes

strategies.title: Respect the Dollar Rebound
strategies.body: A stronger dollar on top of higher oil can become a second tightening channel, especially for multinational earnings and global risk appetite.
strategies.trigger: Dollar strength persists for another few sessions

strategies.title: Track Housing as the Cleanest Spillover Gauge
strategies.body: Mortgage rates are one of the fastest ways this macro shock can hit the real economy, so housing data and rate-sensitive equities matter more here than usual.
strategies.trigger: Mortgage rates stay elevated and housing-linked names weaken
